annotations.label: Position of a point particle

anchor.snippet: The position of a point particle is defined in relation to a coordinate system

annotations.note: Physical notion of a particle's position in a coordinate frame; no such concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Velocity

anchor.snippet: the rate of change of displacement with time, is defined as the derivative of the position

annotations.note: The underlying derivative operation `deriv` exists, but velocity-as-derivative-of-position is not formalized.

annotations.label: Additivity of velocities

anchor.snippet: velocities are directly additive and subtractive

annotations.note: This is a physical statement about Galilean velocity addition; not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relative velocity (vector form)

anchor.snippet: then the velocity of the first object as seen by the second object is

annotations.note: Relative-velocity vector formula is a physics statement with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Acceleration

anchor.snippet: rate of change of velocity, is the derivative of the velocity with respect to time

annotations.note: Higher derivatives (`iteratedDeriv`/`deriv`) exist as infrastructure, but acceleration as a kinematic quantity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Inertial frame

anchor.snippet: An inertial frame is an idealized frame of reference within which an object with zero net force

annotations.note: Physical notion of an inertial reference frame; not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Galilean transformation

anchor.snippet: defines a group transformation known as the Galilean transformation

annotations.note: Grep found no Galilean group/transformation anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Consequences of the Galilean transformation

anchor.snippet: The transformations have the following consequences

annotations.note: Consequences of the (absent) Galilean transformation are not formalized.

annotations.label: Force

anchor.snippet: A force in physics is any action that causes an object's velocity to change

annotations.note: Physical concept of force; no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Newton's second law

anchor.snippet: Either interpretation has the same mathematical consequences, historically known as

annotations.note: F = dp/dt is a physical law not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Momentum

anchor.snippet: is called the ( canonical ) momentum

annotations.note: Physical (canonical) momentum is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equation of motion

anchor.snippet: which is called the equation of motion

annotations.note: Although Mathlib has ODE existence theory, the physical equation of motion itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Motion under friction

anchor.snippet: assume that friction is the only force acting on the particle

annotations.note: Worked physics example of motion under friction; not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Newton's third law

anchor.snippet: B must exert an equal and opposite reaction force

annotations.note: Action–reaction law is a physical principle with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Work done by a constant force

anchor.snippet: by the force is defined as the scalar product of that force and the displacement vector

annotations.note: The scalar (inner) product underlying work exists via `Inner`, but the physical work quantity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Work as a line integral

anchor.snippet: the work done on the particle is given by the line integral

annotations.note: Mathlib has only complex circle/contour integrals, not line integrals of a force field along a path.

annotations.label: Conservative force

anchor.snippet: the force is said to be conservative

annotations.note: No notion of a conservative (path-independent/gradient) vector field is present in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The (1/2)mv² kinetic-energy formula is physics and not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Work–energy theorem

anchor.snippet: the total work W done on the particle as it moves from position

annotations.note: The work–energy theorem relating net work to kinetic-energy change is not formalized.

annotations.label: Potential energy

anchor.snippet: Conservative forces can be expressed as the gradient of a scalar function, known as the potential energy

annotations.note: The `gradient` of a scalar field exists, but potential energy as a physical quantity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Decrease in PE equals increase in KE

anchor.snippet: The decrease in the potential energy is equal to the increase in the kinetic energy

annotations.note: This energy-exchange statement is physics with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Conservation of energy

anchor.snippet: This result is known as conservation of energy and states that the total energy

annotations.note: Conservation of mechanical energy is a physical theorem not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lagrangian mechanics

anchor.snippet: Lagrangian mechanics is a formulation of classical mechanics founded on the stationary-action principle

annotations.note: Lagrangian formulation of mechanics is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stationary action principle

anchor.snippet: The stationary action principle requires that the action functional of the system

annotations.note: No calculus of variations / Euler–Lagrange / action-functional machinery exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hamiltonian mechanics

anchor.snippet: Hamiltonian mechanics replaces (generalized) velocities

annotations.note: Hamiltonian formulation of mechanics is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hamilton's equations

anchor.snippet: the dynamics of a system are governed by Hamilton's equations

annotations.note: Hamilton's canonical equations are not present in Mathlib.
